item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-4716 has been translated to Japanese!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent piece, I love the use of the Daeva tool to show how decadent and selfish the Neo-Sarkists had become.

Quand les pauvres n'auront plus rien à manger, ils mangeront les riches!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Fifthist Recipe.

  • Tom Cruise's Five-Star Steak
  • Edge of Tomorrow.
  • Mission Impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
█.█. ███████'s work has since begun to be re-examined in an unfavorable light. In addition, it is possible that ███████ herself was affected by the consumption of this meal, as she has been attempting to expand upon her previous work, with the derivatives being met with much criticism.

I'm unsure if I love or hate how transparent this reference is. I'm sure there are other references here I'm not getting, but this was a fun little moment in the text.

I may have found an error, though:

9:20: m.

Either way, take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Delightfully gruesome, sickeningly spectacular, and horrifyingly heinous! As a current student in a mythology class, seeing a Sarkic post made my heart dance with glee (not really, though that would make for an interesting recipe). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not going to tag this with Site Crit since I did some of the preliminary crit work on this during an exchange a few days ago.

This piece was a +1 from me. It takes a while to get there but your strength here is in grabbing all of these threads you hang out and pulling them together using a single narrative piece at the end. The added twist/bonus of doing it without actual dialogue is great, and I think it prevents something like this from drifting into 'too purple', which is what has happened to me any time I have attempted a Sarkic thing.

The tone here gets a little close to whacky at times for the subject matter, but I think this walks the line safely on the good side. That's probably just me interpolating details because of the lack of dialogue more than a commentary on your tone of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this one Keter? Is it because there is probably another one out there somewhere, uncontained? It still seems odd that this artifact should be considered anything other than safe, when it's containment procedures currently amount to essentially "leave it in a box and don't mess with it anymore, we're done with it for now".

Also as a bit of input for somebody not familiar with the canon of which this is apparently a part, this one was pretty impenetrable. The actual skip itself seemed fairly straightforward (as skips go), so I was with you through the description and all that. But the log…man, I read through it once normally, then once carefully, then read a few parts again, and it was confusing and odd in the bad way. It wasn't badly done, I just had a strong suspicion that I lacked the context of some larger canon of stories to make any sense of the randomness going on there, and reading the comments seems to confirm that.

If it is just your intention to write this so that people who already know the canon can understand it and the rest of us aren't really meant to get much out of it, I understand that and accept it. I'm no-voting because I think the article is fine if that is the intent. But if you are interested in having it be less completely random for those who aren't versed in the backstory, I think it could benefit from some edits to make things a bit less overwhelming for someone coming in blind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent work again Ihp! Loved this in the draft phase, and loved it again now! Gives one all kinds of ideas for tales relating to Neo-Sarkism interacting with other GOIs as well. Gotta imagine they bought those servants from "someone"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay, it's up! Very much enjoyed reading through it in the draft phase, and I'm excited to see it up.

item.title: Seize the Means of Consumption, Comrade. (Author Commentary)
item.content:encoded:
So I've taken a bit of a dive into the Sarkic cults lately, and I've realized something: the Neo-Sarkics have lost their way with regards to the original intent of their religion. Ion started utilizing Yaldabaoth and flesh-crafting as a tool to overthrow the Daevites, and it seems to have worked. But Neo-Sarkics are content to be a bunch of rich corrupt pricks. It's almost as if there's some commentary there…

I tried my hardest to avert note-at-the-end syndrome here. Hopefully I left enough breadcrumbs, but if not:

Spoilers
– hide block

Gerard Hopworth figured out Neo-Sarkicism as a whole was corrupt after he was tasked with helping Putzman create a method to wipe out his far, far more competent political opponents. He decided to modify the original manuscript in order to target anyone who was wealthy enough to be perceived as a threat. He left the first book behind as a decoy, knowing the Foundation would probably contain it, after creating the second volume, which siphoned text from the first.

He basically wants Sarkicism to go back to its roots of overthrowing an oppressive class, and for his first major atttempt at that, he went after his own family. Maybe pinning open a copy of the Daeva Chronicle with a spear was a bit too on-the-nose, but he's not one for subtlety.

Beyond that, for you Magnus Archives fans out there: yes, Gerard Hopworth is meant to be a reference to Jared Hopworth, who became able to twist flesh and bone after reading a very malevolent volume of literature.

Thanks to CalbiriBold, Dyslexion, Metaphysician, Quixotical, Cerastes, and everyone else who critted this.
